question_en: What is the largest organ in the human body?
context_en: The skin is the largest organ, at about 2 m² and up to 5 kg. It is the first line of defense against the outside.

question_en: How many chromosomes does a human somatic cell have?
context_en: Human somatic cells have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs). Gametes have 23 unpaired (haploid).

question_en: Which organelle is known as the 'powerhouse' of the cell?
context_en: Mitochondria generate ATP through cellular respiration. They have their own DNA, an evolutionary legacy of a symbiotic bacterium.

question_en: Who formulated the laws of genetic inheritance?
context_en: Gregor Mendel published his laws in 1866 studying peas. His work was ignored until the early 20th century.

question_en: Which nitrogenous bases make up DNA?
context_en: DNA contains adenine (A), thymine (T), cytosine (C) and guanine (G). In RNA, thymine is replaced by uracil (U).

question_en: What is the basic unit of life?
context_en: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of living beings, formulated by cell theory in 1839.

question_en: Which type of cell has no defined nucleus?
context_en: Prokaryotes (bacteria and archaea) have no nucleus: their DNA floats freely in the cytoplasm. They appeared ~3,500 million years ago.

question_en: How many bones does the adult human body have?
context_en: An adult has 206 bones. At birth there are ~270 that gradually fuse. The smallest is the stapes in the ear.

question_en: Which system is responsible for defending the body from infections?
context_en: The immune system includes lymphocytes, antibodies and other components that identify and eliminate pathogens.

question_en: Which process allows plants to convert light into energy?
context_en: Photosynthesis converts CO₂ and water into glucose and O₂ using solar energy. It occurs mainly in the chloroplasts.

question_en: Which animal has the largest heart on the planet?
context_en: The blue whale's heart can weigh more than 180 kg and pumps 220 liters of blood per beat. It is the largest known animal.

question_en: Who proposed the theory of evolution by natural selection?
context_en: Darwin published 'On the Origin of Species' in 1859. Alfred Wallace reached similar conclusions independently.

question_en: Which is the strongest muscle in the human body (by proportion)?
context_en: The masseter (of the jaw) exerts the greatest force in proportion to its size: ~90 kg when clenching the molars.

question_en: Which blood type is considered the universal donor?
context_en: O negative has no A, B or Rh antigens, so it can be transfused to anyone in emergencies. AB+ is the universal recipient.

question_en: How many chambers does the human heart have?
context_en: The heart has 4 chambers: 2 atria (top) and 2 ventricles (bottom). Blood flows in pulmonary and systemic circuits.

question_en: In which organelle are proteins synthesized?
context_en: Ribosomes synthesize proteins by reading messenger RNA (translation). They can be free or attached to the endoplasmic reticulum.

question_en: Which is the universal recipient blood group?
context_en: AB+ can receive blood from any group, because it has A, B and Rh antigens and produces no antibodies against them.

question_en: Which neurotransmitter is related to pleasure and reward?
context_en: Dopamine regulates motivation, reward and pleasure. Its deficit is associated with Parkinson's and its excess with schizophrenia.

question_en: Which is the fastest land animal?
context_en: The cheetah reaches 110 km/h in short bursts. Its long body and semi-retractable claws are adaptations for speed.

question_en: Which acid predominates in gastric juice?
context_en: Hydrochloric acid (HCl) in the stomach has a pH of 1-2 and activates pepsin to digest proteins.

question_en: How many legs does an insect have?
context_en: Insects have 6 legs (3 pairs), divided into 3 body segments: head, thorax and abdomen. Arachnids have 8.

question_en: In which part of the brain is balance controlled?
context_en: The cerebellum coordinates movement, balance and posture. It is at the back of the brain.

question_en: Which is the longest bone in the human body?
context_en: The femur (thigh) measures ~46 cm in an average adult. It is also the strongest bone: it supports loads of up to 1,500 kg.

question_en: How many legs do spiders have?
context_en: Spiders have 8 legs, a characteristic of arachnids. Their body is divided into a cephalothorax and abdomen.

question_en: Which organ filters blood and produces urine?
context_en: The kidneys filter ~180 liters of blood a day, eliminating waste and regulating water and electrolytes.

question_en: What is the name of the cell division process that produces gametes?
context_en: Meiosis produces 4 haploid cells from one diploid, with genetic recombination. It is the basis of sexual reproduction.

question_en: Which animal has the largest known genome?
context_en: Some salamanders (like Necturus) have genomes of up to 120,000 Mb, compared to the human's 3,000.

question_en: How many liters of blood does an average adult have?
context_en: An adult has about 5 liters of blood (7-8% of body weight). A loss of more than 20% can be fatal.

question_en: What type of animal is the dolphin?
context_en: Dolphins are cetacean mammals: they breathe air, give birth to live young and nurse them. They evolved from land ancestors.

question_en: Which pigment gives plants their green color?
context_en: Chlorophyll absorbs red and blue light and reflects green. It is essential for photosynthesis and resides in the chloroplasts.

question_en: Which gland regulates metabolism through thyroid hormones?
context_en: The thyroid secretes T3 and T4, which regulate basal metabolism. Its malfunction causes hypo- or hyperthyroidism.

question_en: Who discovered the structure of DNA in 1953?
context_en: Watson and Crick proposed the double helix in 1953, based on Rosalind Franklin's diffraction data. Nobel Prize 1962.

question_en: How many liters of air does a human breathe per day (average)?
context_en: An adult breathes ~11,000 liters of air per day (12-16 breaths per minute, 500 ml each).

question_en: Which organ produces insulin?
context_en: The pancreas, through the islets of Langerhans, produces insulin (regulates glucose) and glucagon. Its dysfunction causes diabetes.

question_en: What is the basic unit of the nervous system?
context_en: The neuron transmits electrical and chemical signals. The human brain has about 86 billion neurons.

question_en: Which taxonomic kingdom includes fungi?
context_en: Fungi form their own kingdom, Fungi. They are not plants: they do not photosynthesize and their cell wall is made of chitin.

question_en: How many senses does the human being traditionally have?
context_en: Traditionally 5 are recognized: sight, hearing, smell, taste and touch. Modern science adds balance, proprioception, etc.

question_en: Which animal has the largest brain?
context_en: The sperm whale has the heaviest brain: up to 8 kg. The human has ~1.4 kg but the highest brain/body ratio among the large ones.

question_en: What type of root does the carrot have?
context_en: The carrot has a taproot (axonomorphic), which grows thickened as a sugar reserve (especially carotenes).

question_en: What type of reproduction do bacteria have?
context_en: Bacteria reproduce by binary fission (asexual), duplicating their DNA and dividing into two identical daughter cells.

question_en: How many vertebrae does the human spine have?
context_en: The spine has 33 vertebrae: 7 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 sacral (fused) and 4 coccygeal (fused).

question_en: What function do red blood cells perform?
context_en: Erythrocytes transport O₂ from the lungs to the tissues thanks to hemoglobin, which contains iron.

question_en: Which animal lays the largest eggs proportionally?
context_en: The kiwi lays eggs at 20% of its body weight, the largest proportionally in the animal kingdom. The ostrich lays the largest in absolute terms.

question_en: In which phase of the cell cycle is DNA replicated?
context_en: DNA replication occurs in the S (synthesis) phase, between G1 and G2 of interphase. M is the mitotic phase.

question_en: Which is the oldest living organism (in years of an individual)?
context_en: Some bristlecone pines (Pinus longaeva) are more than 5,000 years old. The oldest documented, Methuselah, is about 4,855 years old.

question_en: What type of plant is the fern?
context_en: Ferns are pteridophytes: vascular plants without seeds, which reproduce by spores. They have existed since the Devonian.

question_en: Which animal is known as the 'Tasmanian devil'?
context_en: The Tasmanian devil (Sarcophilus harrisii) is a carnivorous marsupial endemic to Tasmania. It is the largest marsupial predator today.

question_en: How many pairs of ribs does a human have?
context_en: The human has 12 pairs of ribs: 7 true, 3 false and 2 floating. They add up to 24 bones in total.

question_en: Which tissue lines internal organs and forms glands?
context_en: Epithelial tissue covers surfaces (skin, mucous membranes) and forms exocrine and endocrine glands. There are 4 basic tissues.

question_en: Which process converts glucose into energy (ATP) in the presence of O₂?
context_en: Aerobic respiration (Krebs cycle + respiratory chain) generates ~36-38 ATP molecules per glucose in the presence of oxygen.

question_en: What type of animal is the whale?
context_en: Whales are marine mammals: they breathe air, are viviparous and nurse their young.

question_en: Which animal is known as the 'king of the jungle'?
context_en: The lion is traditionally called the 'king of the jungle' despite living in savannas, not jungles. It lives in groups called prides.

question_en: Which is the largest living animal on Earth?
context_en: The blue whale can measure more than 30 m and weigh more than 150 tons. It is the largest known animal.

question_en: Which is the most numerous insect on the planet?
context_en: Coleoptera (beetles) are the order with the most described species (>350,000), approximately 40% of insects.

question_en: How many legs does a centipede have?
context_en: Despite the name, centipedes have between 30 and 354 legs depending on the species; always an odd number of pairs.

question_en: Which animal sleeps the most hours per day?
context_en: The brown bat can sleep up to 20 hours a day. Koalas sleep about 18-22 hours.

question_en: Which animal lays the largest eggs in relation to its size?
context_en: The female kiwi lays an egg that can be ¼ of its body weight. In relation to size it is the record.

question_en: Which is the largest amphibian in the world?
context_en: The Chinese giant salamander can measure 1.8 m. It is one of the oldest animals on the planet and is critically endangered.

question_en: What type of animal is the turtle?
context_en: Turtles are reptiles. Some species like the Galápagos ones can live more than 150 years.

question_en: How many hearts does an octopus have?
context_en: The octopus has 3 hearts: one pumps blood to the body and two to the gills. Its blood is blue (copper).

question_en: How many brains does a leech have?
context_en: The leech has 32 brains (ganglia) distributed across its body segments.

question_en: Which animal can regenerate its limbs?
context_en: Starfish, salamanders (and also lizards) have a remarkable regenerative ability.

question_en: Which organ produces bile?
context_en: The liver produces bile, which is stored in the gallbladder and helps digest fats.

question_en: Which organ stores bile?
context_en: The gallbladder stores and concentrates bile until it is needed for digestion.

question_en: What is the main function of the kidneys?
context_en: The kidneys filter the blood, eliminating waste and excess water through urine.

question_en: How many chromosomes does a human gamete have?
context_en: Human gametes (egg and sperm) are haploid: they contain 23 chromosomes, half of a somatic cell.

question_en: What is the structure of DNA?
context_en: DNA has a double-helix structure, described by Watson and Crick in 1953.

question_en: Who published the laws of genetic inheritance?
context_en: Gregor Mendel published his laws in 1866 after experimenting with peas. His works were rediscovered in 1900.

question_en: How many pairs of chromosomes does a human being have?
context_en: Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes (46 in total), including one sex pair (XX or XY).

question_en: Which sex chromosome makes a human embryo genetically male?
context_en: The Y chromosome, present in males (XY), contains the SRY gene that determines male development.

question_en: Which blood cell participates in clotting?
context_en: Platelets (thrombocytes) are cell fragments that participate in blood clotting by forming plugs.

question_en: What type of cells are lymphocytes?
context_en: Lymphocytes are white blood cells (leukocytes) key to the adaptive immune system. There are B, T and NK types.

question_en: Which virus causes AIDS?
context_en: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) attacks the immune system, weakening it until it causes AIDS.

question_en: Which chromosome is involved in Down syndrome?
context_en: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) is due to the presence of an extra chromosome 21 (3 copies instead of 2).

question_en: What is the main function of the lungs?
context_en: The lungs carry out gas exchange: they introduce O₂ into the blood and eliminate CO₂.

question_en: How many pulmonary alveoli does a human have (approx.)?
context_en: Human lungs contain about 300-700 million alveoli, which provide a surface of about 70 m² for gas exchange.

question_en: What is the ribosome?
options_en: The organelle where proteins are synthesized
context_en: Ribosomes are membraneless organelles where proteins are synthesized by translating mRNA.

question_en: Which organelle contains the green pigments in plant cells?
context_en: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll, the green pigment that captures light to carry out photosynthesis.

question_en: Which type of cells form muscle tissue?
context_en: Myocytes (muscle fibers) are the cells of muscle tissue. They can be smooth, striated or cardiac.

question_en: How many types of muscle tissue are there?
context_en: There are three types: smooth (involuntary, organs), striated (skeletal, voluntary) and cardiac (involuntary, heart).

question_en: What type of animal is the spider?
context_en: Spiders are arachnids: they have 8 legs, 2 body segments and lack antennae (unlike insects).

question_en: How many wings do insects typically have?
context_en: Most flying insects have 4 wings (2 pairs). In flies (Diptera) the second pair is reduced to halteres.

question_en: Which pigment in human blood transports oxygen?
context_en: Hemoglobin, present in red blood cells, transports oxygen thanks to its iron content.

question_en: Which hormone do the male gonads produce?
context_en: The testes produce testosterone, the hormone responsible for male sexual characteristics.

question_en: Which glands regulate calcium metabolism?
context_en: Parathyroid hormone (from the parathyroids) and calcitonin (from the thyroid) regulate blood calcium.

question_en: Which virus causes COVID-19?
context_en: COVID-19 is caused by the coronavirus SARS-CoV-2, identified in late 2019 in Wuhan (China).

question_en: What type of relationship is that of the clownfish and the anemone?
context_en: The clownfish and the anemone live in mutualism: the fish receives protection and the anemone gets cleaning and aeration.

question_en: What type of relationship does a louse have with its host?
context_en: The louse is a parasite: it benefits at the expense of the host, which it harms by feeding on its blood.

question_en: Which animal lays eggs and produces milk?
context_en: The platypus (an Australian monotreme) is an egg-laying mammal: it lays eggs but nurses its young.

question_en: How many blood groups are there in the ABO system?
context_en: There are 4 ABO blood groups: A, B, AB and O. In addition, the Rh factor (+/-) is considered.

question_en: Which kingdom includes animals?
context_en: Animalia is one of the kingdoms of biological classification. It includes all multicellular heterotrophic animals.

question_en: Which taxonomic group is made up of unicellular prokaryotic organisms without a nucleus?
context_en: Bacteria are unicellular prokaryotic organisms (without a defined cell nucleus). They belong to the Bacteria domain.

question_en: What process do viruses use to multiply?
context_en: Viruses do not multiply on their own: they must infect cells and use their machinery to replicate.

question_en: What type of leaves do conifers have?
context_en: Conifers have needle-like or scaly leaves. E.g.: pines, firs.

question_en: How many petals does a lily flower have?
context_en: Lily flowers have 6 petals (actually, 3 petals and 3 similar sepals), forming a hexapetalous calyx.

question_en: Which pigment gives venous blood its red color?
context_en: Venous blood is darker because hemoglobin without oxygen (deoxyhemoglobin) is a darker red.

question_en: What is the structural unit of the kidney?
context_en: The nephron is the functional unit of the kidney. There are about 1 million nephrons per human kidney.

question_en: What type of symmetry do jellyfish have?
context_en: Jellyfish have radial symmetry: their parts are distributed around a central axis.

question_en: What type of symmetry do humans have?
context_en: Humans (and most complex animals) have bilateral symmetry: the left side is a mirror image of the right.

question_en: Which is the smallest bone in the human body?
context_en: The stapes, in the middle ear, measures ~3 mm. It is the smallest bone in the human body.

question_en: Which bone forms the hip along with the ilium and the ischium?
context_en: The hip bone (coxal bone) is formed by the fusion of the ilium, ischium and pubis.

question_en: How many valves does the human heart have?
context_en: The heart has 4 valves: tricuspid, pulmonary, mitral and aortic. They prevent backflow between chambers and arteries.

question_en: Which gland produces melatonin?
context_en: The pineal gland (epiphysis) produces melatonin, a hormone that regulates the sleep-wake cycle.

question_en: Which is the main inhibitory neurotransmitter of the CNS?
context_en: GABA (gamma-aminobutyric acid) is the main inhibitory neurotransmitter of the central nervous system.

question_en: Which neurotransmitter is involved in Parkinson's?
context_en: Parkinson's is due to the degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra of the brain.

question_en: Which virus causes chickenpox?
context_en: The varicella-zoster virus (VZV), a herpesvirus, causes chickenpox and, upon reactivation, shingles ('herpes zoster').

question_en: Which animal can live up to 200 years?
context_en: The Galápagos giant tortoise can live more than 150-200 years. 'Lonesome George' died at about 100 years old in 2012.

question_en: Which 'immortal' animal can reverse its life cycle?
context_en: The jellyfish Turritopsis dohrnii can revert to a polyp state, making it biologically 'immortal'.

question_en: How many eggs does a queen bee lay per day approximately?
context_en: A queen bee lays about 1,500-2,000 eggs a day during the active season.

question_en: How many legs do crustaceans typically have?
context_en: Decapods (crustaceans like crabs, lobsters) have 10 legs (5 pairs).

question_en: Which organ produces the hormones FSH and LH?
context_en: The anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis) produces the hormones FSH and LH, gonadotropins that regulate the reproductive system.

question_en: Which is the longest muscle in the human body?
context_en: The sartorius, in the thigh, is the longest muscle in the human body. It measures about 50-60 cm.

question_en: What type of animal is the snail?
context_en: Snails are gastropod mollusks. They carry their coiled shell and move on a muscular foot.

question_en: Which organ produces pulmonary surfactant?
context_en: Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant, a substance that reduces surface tension in the pulmonary alveoli.

question_en: What defines a mammal?
context_en: Mammals are characterized by having hair and mammary glands to nurse their young. Most are viviparous.

question_en: Which group of birds cannot fly?
context_en: Ratites include the ostrich, emu, rhea, kiwi and cassowary. All flightless.

question_en: How many species of mammals are there approximately?
context_en: Approximately 6,500 species of living mammals have been described. Half are rodents.

question_en: How many species of birds are there approximately?
context_en: About 11,000 species of living birds are known in the world.

question_en: Which animal is considered the most venomous in the world?
context_en: The box jellyfish (Chironex fleckeri), an Australian jellyfish, is considered the most venomous animal. It can kill a human in minutes.

question_en: What is the main function of sperm?
context_en: Sperm are the male gametes. Their function is to fertilize the egg, contributing half of the genetic material.

question_en: Where does spermatogenesis take place?
context_en: Spermatogenesis occurs in the seminiferous tubules of the testes.

question_en: Where does human fertilization usually occur?
context_en: Fertilization normally occurs in the Fallopian tube (tubal ampulla), after ovulation.

question_en: How long does a human pregnancy last (weeks)?
context_en: A human pregnancy lasts approximately 40 weeks (~9 months) from the last menstrual period.

question_en: What type of animal is the frog?
context_en: Frogs are anuran amphibians. They spend part of their life in water (tadpoles) and part on land.

question_en: What type of reproduction do most amphibians have?
context_en: Most amphibians are oviparous: they lay eggs (usually in water) with external fertilization.

question_en: Which is the largest land animal?
context_en: The African savanna elephant is the largest land animal, with males that can weigh 6 tons.

question_en: Which is the tallest animal in the world?
context_en: The giraffe can reach 5.5 m in height. Its neck has the same 7 cervical vertebrae as most mammals.

question_en: What type of leaves does a deciduous tree lose?
context_en: Deciduous trees lose all their leaves at the end of autumn and renew them in spring.

question_en: Which type of tree keeps its leaves all year round?
context_en: Evergreen trees keep their leaves all year round, renewing them gradually.

question_en: Which process converts DNA into RNA?
context_en: Transcription is the process by which a strand of DNA is copied into messenger RNA (mRNA).

question_en: Which process converts mRNA into protein?
context_en: Translation is the process by which ribosomes read the mRNA and synthesize a chain of amino acids (a protein).

question_en: How many essential amino acids exist for humans?
context_en: There are 9 essential amino acids for humans: they must be obtained from the diet. In total there are 20 proteinogenic amino acids.

question_en: How many pairs of cranial nerves does a human have?
context_en: We have 12 pairs of cranial nerves (olfactory, optic, etc.), numbered with Roman numerals from I to XII.

question_en: Which organ regulates body temperature?
context_en: The hypothalamus is the body's thermostat: it regulates body temperature and many other homeostatic functions.

question_en: Which part of the brain coordinates movement?
context_en: The cerebellum coordinates movement, balance and posture. Its name means 'little brain'.

question_en: How many neurons does the human brain have approximately?
context_en: The human brain contains about 86 billion neurons. Each one can have thousands of connections (synapses).

question_en: Which hormone controls blood glucose levels when they drop?
context_en: Glucagon raises blood glucose by releasing glucose from the liver. It is antagonistic to insulin.

question_en: Which organ produces glucagon?
context_en: The pancreas (alpha cells of the islets of Langerhans) produces glucagon. The beta cells produce insulin.

question_en: What type of molecule is insulin?
context_en: Insulin is a protein (a peptide hormone) of 51 amino acids produced by the beta cells of the pancreas.

question_en: Which cycle describes the flow of nitrogen between organisms and the environment?
context_en: The nitrogen cycle describes how this element passes through the atmosphere, soil, organisms and water through processes like fixation, nitrification and denitrification.

question_en: Which process allows bacteria to fix atmospheric nitrogen?
context_en: Some bacteria (like Rhizobium in legume roots) can fix atmospheric N₂, converting it into ammonia.

question_en: What type of relationship is that of the rhinoceros and the cattle egret?
context_en: The cattle egret and the rhinoceros (or the buffalo) have mutualism: the egret eats the larger animal's parasites, benefiting both.

question_en: What adaptation does the camel have for the desert?
context_en: The camel's hump stores fat, which it metabolizes to obtain energy and water. Its feet are wide so as not to sink into the sand.

question_en: Which organ produces antibodies?
context_en: B lymphocytes (a type of white blood cell) produce antibodies when they differentiate into plasma cells.

question_en: Which cellular process produces energy in the absence of O₂?
context_en: Fermentation produces ATP in the absence of oxygen. There are several types: lactic, alcoholic, etc.

question_en: Which sex chromosomes do XX women have?
context_en: Women are typically XX (two X chromosomes). Men are XY.

question_en: How many pairs of spinal nerves does a human being have?
context_en: The human being has 31 pairs of spinal nerves that emerge from the spinal cord: 8 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 sacral and 1 coccygeal.

question_en: What is the cause of iron-deficiency anemia?
context_en: Iron-deficiency anemia is caused by a lack of iron, which is needed to synthesize hemoglobin.

question_en: What effect does growth hormone (GH) have?
context_en: Growth hormone, produced by the pituitary, stimulates the growth of bones and tissues.

question_en: Which type of nerve tissue insulates axons?
context_en: The myelin sheath, formed by Schwann cells (peripheral system) or oligodendrocytes (central), insulates axons, speeding up transmission.

question_en: Which animal has the highest number of teeth?
context_en: Snails can have tens of thousands of microscopic teeth (the radula). It is the highest 'teeth' count in the animal kingdom.
